worth_it_explanation: Block lists decide which advertisers, advertiser categories, and creative domains a publisher will not accept, so they sit directly on the line between protecting the brand and earning revenue. A list that is too narrow lets unwanted creatives through. A list that is too broad turns away demand the publisher would have been paid for, and the lost revenue does not show up as an error anywhere. The check is a review of the block lists a publisher already maintains in PubMatic, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete list the publisher can tighten or trim.
intro: Creative blocking is the set of controls a publisher uses to keep specific advertisers, advertiser categories, and creative domains off their inventory. In PubMatic these controls live in the supply-side platform (SSP) configuration, and they map to the OpenRTB blocking attributes that travel with each bid request, such as blocked advertiser domains (badv) and blocked advertiser categories (bcat). This subcategory is about whether those block lists reflect the publisher's own brand standards and whether they are scoped tightly enough to do their job without suppressing demand the publisher meant to keep. These checks confirm the lists are maintained deliberately rather than left at a default, and that the blocking is matched to a real standard rather than applied so broadly that it costs fill.

audit_checks.title: Advertiser, category, and domain block lists do not match the publisher's brand standards
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Block lists that do not reflect the publisher's actual brand standards let creatives through that the publisher would reject, or leave gaps a competitor or a sensitive category can slip into. The exposure is a brand-safety incident on the publisher's own pages, which can damage the relationship with direct advertisers and audience, so the lists need to track the standard the publisher means to enforce.

educate.base:
Creative blocking in a supply-side platform (SSP) is how a publisher tells the auction which ads it will not accept. The controls fall into a few groups. A blocked advertiser domains list (badv in OpenRTB terms) names the landing-page domains a publisher refuses, which is how a publisher keeps a direct competitor or a specific brand off its pages. A blocked advertiser categories list (bcat) names categories the publisher will not run, and those categories come from the Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy, a shared vocabulary that lets the publisher, the SSP, and the demand side all describe content the same way. Creative-domain and creative-attribute controls cover the rest, such as blocking a specific creative or an attribute like audio that plays on load.

The audit question is not whether these lists exist. It is whether they match the standard the publisher actually means to enforce. A publisher with a stated policy against gambling, alcohol, or political advertising, or a list of named competitors it will not run, has a brand standard. The block lists in PubMatic are the place that standard becomes real. When the lists drift from the policy, two things happen. Creatives the publisher would reject clear the auction because no rule stops them, and the publisher only finds out when someone notices the ad on the page.

Block lists also go stale. Advertiser domains change, new sensitive categories appear in the taxonomy, and a competitor the publisher meant to block launches under a new domain. A list set up once and left alone slowly stops matching the brands and categories it was meant to cover. The IAB has added a Sensitive Topics branch to its Content Taxonomy precisely so publishers can describe high-risk content categories in a consistent way, which only helps if the publisher's bcat list is kept current against it.

A few signs point to block lists that do not match the publisher's standards:
  • A written brand-safety policy exists, but the badv and bcat lists in PubMatic do not contain the advertisers or categories the policy names
  • The lists were configured at launch and have no record of review since, while the policy or the competitive set has changed
  • The publisher cannot say which IAB taxonomy version its category blocks are written against, so it is unclear whether newer sensitive categories are covered
You may want to confirm that the block lists in PubMatic are a current expression of the publisher's brand standards. One approach is to put the written policy and the live lists side by side and reconcile them, then set a review cadence so the lists track the policy over time.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, editorial credibility is part of the product, so a creative from a category the newsroom would not endorse, or from a brand the publisher has a direct conflict with, carries more cost than the lost impression. You may want to reconcile the bcat and badv lists against the publication's advertising policy first because the categories a news or lifestyle brand treats as off-limits are often the ones the policy is most explicit about.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a direct retail competitor advertising next to the publisher's own product content is a real conflict, so the blocked advertiser domains list matters as much as the category blocks. You may want to confirm the badv list names the competing retailers and marketplaces the publisher does not want on its commercial pages, and that it is updated when a new competitor enters the space.

learn_more.source_title: ORTB2 Blocking Module for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed configuration documentation for advertiser, category, and creative block lists is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the publisher's written brand-safety policy, then read the live advertiser, category, and domain block lists in PubMatic to see whether the lists name what the policy names.

pubmatic_ui.label: PubMatic publisher console
steps: Locate the publisher's written brand-safety or advertising policy, and list the advertisers, advertiser categories, and creative types it says should be blocked.
Open the block-list configuration in the PubMatic console and record the blocked advertiser domains, the blocked categories, and any creative-level blocks in place.
Note which IAB Content Taxonomy version the category blocks are written against, so it is clear whether newer sensitive categories are covered.
Reconcile the two lists and record any advertiser or category the policy names that the live block lists do not cover, and any block in place that the policy does not call for.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the publisher's written brand standard alongside the live advertiser, category, and creative block lists in PubMatic. A setup where the lists name the advertisers and categories the policy calls for, written against a current taxonomy version, passes. A setup where the policy names advertisers or categories that the live lists do not cover, or where the lists have no record of review against a changed policy, is a fail recorded with the specific gaps between the policy and the lists.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the publisher's brand-safety policy and the live block lists above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the advertiser, category, and creative block lists for {{site_name}} in PubMatic do not match the brand-safety policy the publisher means to enforce. Block lists that drift from the policy let creatives through that the publisher would reject, and the gap usually surfaces only when someone notices the ad on the page. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling the written policy against the live blocked advertiser domains (badv) and blocked advertiser categories (bcat) in PubMatic, confirming the category blocks are written against a current Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy version, and closing any gap where the policy names an advertiser or category the lists do not cover. After the reconciliation, a regular review keeps the lists tracking the policy as advertisers and categories change.

audit_checks.title: Block lists are over-broad and suppress demand the publisher would have accepted
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An over-broad block list turns away demand the publisher would have been paid for, and the lost revenue does not show up as an error because the impression simply went unfilled or cleared lower. Blocking a wide category to catch a few unwanted creatives, or carrying duplicate blocks from several tools, can cut fill and price across a slice of inventory the publisher never meant to restrict.

educate.base:
A block list protects the brand, and every entry on it also removes demand from the auction. That trade is the point, but it only pays off when the entries are scoped to what the publisher actually wants to keep out. The failure mode here is the opposite of a missing block. It is a list so wide that it turns away demand the publisher would happily have accepted, with no signal that it is happening because a blocked impression does not register as an error. It either goes unfilled or clears at a lower price from whatever demand remains.

Over-broad blocking takes a few common shapes. A publisher blocks a whole top-level category in the Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy to catch one sensitive subtopic, and in doing so removes every advertiser in the broader category. A blocked advertiser domains list grows over time as names are added and never removed, so it carries entries for advertisers that no longer exist or were never a real conflict. Blocking also stacks across layers. A publisher may set blocks in PubMatic, again in a verification vendor, and again at the demand-side, so the same category is filtered several times and the combined effect is wider than any single list looks.

The OpenRTB blocking attributes that carry these lists have no hard length limit, which means there is no natural pressure to keep them tight. Prebid's own guidance on the blocking module notes that a longer blocked list raises the chance of request timeouts, so length has a cost beyond the demand it removes. The point of the review is to weigh each block against the demand it filters, keeping the ones the policy needs and trimming the ones that cost more in lost fill than they return in protection.

A few signs point to block lists that are over-broad:
  • A whole top-level taxonomy category is blocked when the publisher's concern is one narrow subtopic within it
  • The blocked advertiser domains list has grown long with no removal process, so it likely carries stale or unnecessary entries
  • Reporting shows lower fill or price on inventory that runs the block lists, compared with comparable inventory that does not, pointing to demand the blocks are removing
You may want to confirm that each block earns its place against the demand it filters. One approach is to read fill rate and average price on inventory under the block lists against comparable inventory, then narrow any category block to the specific subtopic and prune stale domain entries.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a broad category block such as the entire news or politics branch can remove a large share of contextually relevant demand, which is often the demand that pays best on editorial inventory. You may want to narrow such a block to the specific sensitive subtopic the policy targets rather than the whole branch, so the publication keeps the relevant advertisers it has no reason to reject.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, blocking a broad retail or shopping category to keep out a few direct competitors can also remove complementary advertisers whose products sit alongside the publisher's own without conflict. You may want to block the specific competing domains rather than the whole category, so the commercial inventory keeps the non-competing demand that values that audience.

learn_more.source_title: ORTB2 Blocking Module for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed configuration documentation for advertiser, category, and creative block lists is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the scope of each block against the demand it removes, and read fill and price on inventory under the block lists against comparable inventory to see whether the blocking is wider than the policy needs.

pubmatic_ui.label: PubMatic publisher console
steps: Open the block-list configuration in the PubMatic console and record each blocked category, noting whether a whole top-level taxonomy category is blocked when the policy concern is a narrower subtopic.
Read the blocked advertiser domains list and note its length and whether any removal or review process exists because a long list with no pruning likely carries stale entries.
Check whether the same categories are also blocked in a verification vendor or at the demand-side, so the combined blocking is wider than the PubMatic list alone.

pubmatic_reporting.label: PubMatic reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the inventory that runs the block lists, with fill rate, revenue, and average eCPM.
Compare that inventory against comparable inventory with lighter or no blocking to see whether the blocks are removing demand and lowering fill or price.
Note any case where a broad category block lines up with a drop in fill or price, which points to demand the block could keep if it were narrowed to the specific subtopic.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the scope of each block alongside the fill and price effect on the inventory it covers. A setup where each block is scoped to the specific advertiser or subtopic the policy targets, with no stale domain entries and no large unexplained fill or price gap against comparable inventory, passes. A setup where a broad category block or a long stale domain list lines up with lower fill or price on the affected inventory is a fail recorded with the specific blocks that cost more demand than the policy requires.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the scope of each block and the fill and price effect on the inventory it covers above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the block lists for {{site_name}} in PubMatic are broader than the brand-safety policy requires, so they remove demand the publisher would have accepted. An over-broad block turns away paying demand without registering as an error because the impression goes unfilled or clears lower from the demand that remains. {{voice_recommend}} narrowing any whole-category block to the specific Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy subtopic the policy targets, pruning stale entries from the blocked advertiser domains (badv) list, and reading fill and price on the affected inventory against comparable inventory to confirm each block earns its place. After the change, recheck fill rate and average price to confirm the narrower lists recovered demand without weakening the protection the policy needs.
